summary: Patrick McKenzie explains why he's abandoning desktop software development in favor of web applications, based on concrete data from running both versions of Bingo Card Creator simultaneously. The web version converts trial users to paying customers at nearly double the rate of the desktop version, despite having fewer features and only two months of development versus three years. Web apps eliminate the lengthy shareware download funnel, drastically reduce support burden, enable real-time A/B testing and analytics, and make piracy essentially impossible. The cumulative advantages in conversion rates, customer acquisition costs, development velocity, and per-user customization make web apps strictly dominant for his business.
key_points: The shareware download funnel has 17 discrete steps where customers can drop off, while web apps eliminate most of them, resulting in nearly 2x better trial-to-purchase conversion rates
Better conversion rates cut AdWords cost per acquisition from $20 to $9, creating a structural competitive advantage against rivals selling desktop software
Web apps reduced support requests from 15 per 50 customers to 3, by eliminating installation issues, lost registration keys, and outdated software versions

Analytics on the web app revealed counterintuitive insights: the most common use case was baby showers (not what he expected), more features actually hurt sales, and most customers purchase within two hours of signup
Web apps enable per-user customization and rapid A/B testing — 67 updates deployed in 7 weeks as a side project, versus months for desktop distribution
money_quotes: There is no customer support experience so good as avoiding the problem in the first place.
Theoretical utility of desktop software is one thing, but I can't ignore what my numbers are telling me.
The more features I add to the software, the worse it sells. This is, needless to say, highly unintuitive to most software developers.
Most customers purchase within two hours of signup, so it is absolutely imperative that their first use of the software exceed all their expectations.
I have released 67 updates to Bingo Card Creator in the past 7 weeks, and this isn't even my day job.
A button here, some textual edits there, seven A/B tests, etc etc, and pretty soon you're looking at the magic of compounding 1% improvements.
At the end of the day, I'm an engineer. I follow the numbers where they lead me.
key_insight: Web applications structurally outperform desktop software for small businesses because they compress the sales funnel, enable rapid experimentation, and produce data that reveals counterintuitive truths about customer behavior.
